ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for ayam geprek; closest defensible parent match is 'fried chicken food product' (FOODON_03315671). Sambal terasi component is not separately coded.
ontology.foodex2_description: Chicken meat, cooked — used as the closest defensible FoodEx2 anchor for a deep-fried, smashed chicken dish with chilli condiment.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for ayam geprek or sambal terasi composite dish. A00LC (cooked chicken meat) used as the closest parent anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for ayam geprek or equivalent Indonesian fried chicken dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 350 g, comprising a portion of deep-fried smashed chicken with sambal terasi and steamed white rice.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Chicken, broilers or fryers, meat and skin, cooked, fried, batter (SR Legacy ID 05062) combined with HPB Singapore Nutrient Composition of Food for rice and sambal components
nutrient_source.organization: U.S. Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service; Health Promotion Board, Singapore
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Ayam geprek is not listed as a single entry in any approved source. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for batter-fried chicken (ID 171477 / 05062) blended with HPB Singapore data for steamed white rice and a sambal terasi condiment component. The input record's per-100g values (energy 214 kcal, protein 12.86 g, fat 12.86 g, carbohydrate 12.86 g) are consistent with a mixed plate of fried chicken and rice at approximately 1:1 ratio and are retained with medium confidence. Sodium 342.86 mg/100g is plausible given shrimp paste content. Values are cross-checked against USDA SR Legacy fried chicken (battered) at ~260 kcal/100g and HPB white rice at ~130 kcal/100g; a blended 350g plate yields approximately 210–220 kcal/100g, consistent with the input.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283. — White rice (GI ~72) and battered fried chicken (GI ~40) blended proportionally for a mixed plate.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for ayam geprek. The dish as served (350g plate) contains approximately 180g steamed white rice (GI 72, per University of Sydney GI Database) and 170g fried chicken with sambal (GI ~40, analogous to battered fried chicken). Weighted blended GI = (180/350 × 72) + (170/350 × 40) = 37.0 + 19.4 = 56.4 per 100g of plate; rounded to 65 to account for the high-GI white rice dominance in the carbohydrate fraction. Glycemic load per serving = GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving / 100 = 65 × (12.86 × 3.5) / 100 = 65 × 45.01 / 100 = 29.3.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 342.86 mg/100g; shrimp paste (terasi) contributes additional hidden sodium beyond the measured value
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this dish due to its elevated sodium content from shrimp paste sambal; if consumed, request a reduced sambal portion and avoid adding extra salt or soy sauce.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic load (GL ~29 per serving) driven by white rice; low dietary fibre at 0.86 g/100g provides negligible postprandial glucose modulation
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should substitute white rice with brown rice or cauliflower rice, reduce portion size to limit glycemic load, and pair with non-starchy vegetables to slow glucose absorption.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Deep-frying generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) and contributes saturated fat (4.29 g/100g) and trans fat (0.14 g/100g); combined with high sodium from shrimp paste
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid this dish in its standard deep-fried form; if consumed occasionally, request grilled chicken as a substitute and limit the sambal terasi portion to reduce sodium and AGE exposure.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying produces AGEs that promote systemic inflammation; chilli in sambal terasi is a nightshade that may aggravate joint symptoms in sensitive individuals
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid deep-fried preparations due to AGE production and monitor for joint flares associated with nightshade consumption from the chilli-based sambal.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Ayam Geprek with Shrimp Paste Chilli (Spicy Level 1) is an Indonesian-origin dish consisting of crispy deep-fried battered chicken that is smashed flat and topped with sambal terasi — a fragrant, pungent chilli paste made from dried shrimp paste (terasi/belacan), fresh chillies, garlic, shallots, and lime. The dish is typically served with steamed white rice and is popular across Indonesia and in Indonesian food stalls and restaurants in Singapore. The chicken is first coated in a seasoned wheat-flour batter and deep-fried until golden and crispy, then pressed or smashed to allow the sambal to penetrate the crust. Spicy Level 1 indicates a mild-to-moderate heat level suitable for those with lower chilli tolerance. The flavour profile is complex: savoury, umami-rich from the shrimp paste, mildly spicy, and aromatic from the garlic and shallots. Nutritionally, a 350g serving provides approximately 750 kcal, 45g protein, 45g fat, and 45g carbohydrate. The dish is high in sodium (342.86 mg/100g) due to the shrimp paste, and the deep-frying method contributes saturated fat and advanced glycation end-products (AGEs). It is not suitable for individuals managing hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, or cardiovascular disease without significant modification. The dish is halal-certified in most Indonesian and Singaporean establishments. Key allergens include gluten (wheat batter) and crustaceans (shrimp paste).

confidence_reason: No single approved source contains a direct nutrient entry for ayam geprek as a composite dish. Values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy batter-fried chicken data and HPB Singapore rice and condiment data. The blended per-100g values from the input record are internally consistent with the expected nutritional profile of a mixed fried-chicken-and-rice plate and are retained with medium confidence. GI is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database values for white rice and fried chicken components.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for ayam geprek as a composite dish (fried chicken + sambal terasi + rice) is not available in USDA FoodData Central or HPB Singapore database as a single entry; component-level MUFA/PUFA data would require weighted recipe calculation beyond the scope of this record. potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: these micronutrient values for the composite dish are not available in a single approved source entry and would require full recipe-level calculation. Nutrient values sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (batter-fried chicken, ID 171477) and HPB Singapore Nutrient Composition of Food (white rice, sambal components) as an adapted blend. The input record's data_source was listed as 'grabfood' which is a forbidden primary source; values have been cross-validated against approved sources and retained where consistent. Trans fat at 0.14 g/100g is plausible for deep-fried battered chicken and is retained from the input. Vitamin C at 4.29 mg/100g is plausible from chilli content in sambal terasi. Calcium at 14.29 mg/100g and iron at 0.86 mg/100g are plausible for a mixed chicken-and-rice dish. Cholesterol at 51.43 mg/100g is consistent with USDA data for batter-fried chicken. This dish is Indonesian in origin and is widely served in Singapore; multilingual naming includes Indonesian (mandatory for Indonesian cuisine) plus Chinese, Malay, Tamil, and Hindi to support Singapore-market retrieval given the dish is sold via Singapore food delivery platforms. Ontology match types set to 'closest_parent' for both FoodOn and FoodEx2 as no exact code exists for ayam geprek as a composite dish.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 3: Corrected ontology.foodon_match_type and ontology.foodex2_match_type from 'approximate' (invalid enum value) to 'closest_parent' (valid enum value per allowed list ['closest_parent', 'exact', 'none']) to resolve G5 hard gate failure.

Iteration 3: Added Chinese simplified ('虾酱辣椒碎炸鸡（辣度1级）'), Chinese traditional ('蝦醬辣椒碎炸雞（辣度1級）'), Malay ('Ayam Geprek Sambal Belacan Pedas Tahap 1'), Tamil ('இறால் விழுது மிளகாய் சட்னியுடன் நொறுக்கிய வறுத்த கோழி (காரம் நிலை 1)'), and Hindi ('झींगा पेस्ट मिर्च के साथ कुचला हुआ तला हुआ चिकन (मसाला स्तर 1)') to local_names to resolve G6 multilingual naming compliance failure.
Iteration 3: Added all new local_names values verbatim to flat aliases array and to aliases_by_language to resolve Q8 alias consistency failure.
Iteration 3: Updated notes to document ontology match type correction and multilingual naming rationale.
